tldr: Headlines across Europe, Asia-Pacific, and wire services announce David Hockney's death at 88 with near-universal framing as a British cultural titan of contemporary and pop art; Le Parisien alone foregrounds his auction-record market value, and Brazil's articles supply no Hockney coverage at all.

evidence.quote: Celebrated British contemporary artist David Hockney
Il a marqué l'art contemporain de son empreinte
era il più celebre pittore contemporaneo
Legendary British painter David Hockney dies at 88
David Hockney, of the most celebrated artists
artist renowned for his pool scenes, dies aged 88
David Hockney, revolutionary British artist, dies aged 88
peintre « le plus cher du monde »

frames.sub_frame: Personal joy of place — Normandy life

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to frame Hockney's death through market value — 'le plus cher du monde' (world's most expensive painter) — turning an obituary headline into an ECONOMIC frame where every other outlet runs a purely cultural tribute.

Only outlet to use 'revolutionary' as the lead descriptor, framing Hockney's death as the end of an artistic rupture rather than the loss of a famous figure; other outlets reach for status superlatives ('greatest', 'legendary', 'most celebrated') but not transformation.

Only outlet to surface a personal-life angle at headline level — reprising a 2022 encounter in Normandy ('Vivre ici, c'est fantastique') — foregrounding Hockney's lived happiness and sense of place rather than artistic rank or death fact.

bottom_line: The headline layer positions Hockney's death as the loss of a British cultural titan — 'revolutionary', 'legendary', 'the most celebrated' — with Le Parisien alone surfacing the market-record angle that body-text analyses will likely expand on.
